lesson: Lead Forry mirror: standard line is Spikes, but Hidden Power Forry exists as a lure set. Treat Spikes as DEFAULT, not certain.

Lead Forry mirror: opponent may switch to a Rapid Spinner (Starmie) instead of racing Spikes — preempts opp Spikes by bringing in Spin first. Consider double-switch reads when both leads are Forretress.
Forretress vs incoming Starmie: pivoting to a Snorlax-class attacker is often better than racing Spikes when the opp Spinner is already on the field. The Spikes will get spun anyway.
TWave-Starmie set exists in 1500-elo. When opp's Starmie has 1+ free turn vs a Normal-type incoming, Thunder Wave is strictly better than offensive coverage for stall strategies.
Para'd Snorlax facing Starmie that just TWaved: Lovely Kiss is high-EV but switching back to Forretress is the safer line when you expect opp to also switch to set Spikes. Mutual scout.
HIT: Starmie pivots back to Forretress once the para job is done; the Spikes race resumes. Match: pro pivoted exactly as predicted.
HIT: Forry mirror with revealed pivots, both at 100%, predicted Spikes was correct on p1. Reinforce: in Forry-vs-Forry turn 4 after mutual scouting, Spikes is the standard line.

Forry mirror after p1 has revealed it commits to Spikes-setter role: p2's better line may be pre-Spin via Starmie pivot rather than racing the second Spikes layer (forcing p1 to commit further while p2 keeps Spin live).

Snorlax vs Blissey lead: pivot to a Ground attacker (Rhydon Earthquake) breaks Blissey 2HKO. Setup with Curse is a slower line; aggressive break may be the pro choice when a hard-counter is available on the team.

HIT: Blissey vs Snorlax lead, Toxic is the strong-prior call — Snorlax usually has no recovery in Gen 2 OU, Toxic puts it on an 8-turn timer. Toxic into an incoming Rhydon also lands; only Steel/Poison types are immune.

alternative_hypotheses: Just a scout switch (rejected — Umbreon would not be the scout pick if so; Skarmory or another safer pivot would be used)
Sleep-eat sacrifice (rejected — Umbreon is too valuable defensively to sack to Lovely Kiss when Snorlax could absorb instead)
investigation.future_turn_evidence: Turn 5: Umbreon used Pursuit on Jynx, marked super-effective (Dark vs Psychic), 22% damage. Turn 4: Umbreon ate first Ice Beam at 75%; turn 5 second Ice Beam dropped to 56% but the Pursuit landed. The Pursuit-trap thesis is directly confirmed.
investigation.root_cause_hypothesis: Umbreon is the team's dedicated Jynx Pursuit-trapper. The switch t1 brings the trap into the most common Jynx lead opener (Lovely Kiss). Even if Jynx Ice Beams instead, Umbreon's bulk + Leftovers absorb 2 hits before getting low; Pursuit on the eventual Jynx switch removes the sleeper. Standard Gen 2 OU anti-Jynx package.

lesson: Lead Snorlax vs Jynx with Pursuit-trapper on team: switch IMMEDIATELY to Umbreon/Tyranitar to trap Jynx. Eating 2 Ice Beams (~44%) on the trapper is worth removing Jynx from the game. Lovely Kiss race is the wrong line because both sides can sleep, neutralizing the advantage.

alternative_hypotheses: Just cycling to Spikes setter (rejected — Jynx had a free turn to sleep something; only worth giving up if there's a specific reason)
Anti-Snorlax Self-Destruct fear (low confidence — Self-Destruct turn 1 vs Jynx would be unusual)
investigation.future_turn_evidence: Turn 5: Jynx was Pursuit-trapped anyway (came back turn 3, ate Umbreon's Pursuit at 84% turn 5). So the scout-pivot was correct strategy but the follow-up was bad — bringing Jynx back too early. Turn 12: Cloyster used Spikes, confirming it's the team's Spikes setter (additional value behind the scout-pivot).
investigation.root_cause_hypothesis: Jynx is uniquely vulnerable to Pursuit (Dark-type STAB hits 4x: Dark>Psychic 2x and STAB 1.5x; not quite 4x but close). Smart opponents bring Pursuit-trappers vs Jynx leads. Switching Jynx out turn 1 scouts the opp; if p1 brings the trapper, Cloyster absorbs the Pursuit instead.

lesson: Lead Jynx vs Snorlax: anticipate Pursuit-trap switch (Umbreon/Tyranitar) from p1; pivot to a bulky resist (Cloyster, Forretress) on turn 1 to scout, bring Jynx back when the trapper is out. Not switching = walking into the trap, losing Jynx for nothing.

alternative_hypotheses: Just avoiding Counter (rejected — if that were the only fear, p1 would Rock Slide instead, which can't be Countered as effectively)
Scout for Bliss's move (rejected — Bliss already revealed Toxic turn 1; nothing more to scout)
investigation.future_turn_evidence: Turn 3: p1 Cloyster used Spikes (layer 1 on p2). Turn 4: p2 Cloyster set their own Spikes; p1 Cloyster used Toxic into the mirror. Turn 5: p2 switched Bengar (Gengar) in and took 12% from Spikes immediately — the hazard cost p2 their first switch-in. The Spikes-race ROI was high.
investigation.root_cause_hypothesis: p1 traded a free Bliss turn for a Spikes layer. The Cloyster pivot is risky (takes Toxic, has no recovery) but the Spikes layer is permanent until spun. Hazard-race over single-target breaks is a known Gen 2 OU principle on stall vs balanced matchups.

lesson: Rhydon vs Blissey free turn: don't auto-Earthquake. Bliss can't break ANY switch-in for one turn, so the EV-maximizing line is to bring in your Spikes setter (Cloyster) on the free pivot. Setting Spikes during a free Bliss turn buys hazard infrastructure that ticks the opp's entire team. The Earthquake KO is available later when the same matchup recurs.

alternative_hypotheses: Counter for a perceived attacker (rejected — opponent's lead was Snorlax, but switched to Rhydon then Cloyster; the actual attacker hasn't entered)
Sing/Thunder Wave instead (lower-EV — Toxic is permanent damage; sleep wakes up)
investigation.future_turn_evidence: Turn 4: p1 Cloyster at 89% from poison. Turn 5: Cloyster bailed at low HP, never used Surf or any offensive coverage; confirms Cloyster was the setter, not the attacker. Counter would have been wasted (no damage to reflect).
investigation.root_cause_hypothesis: Bliss's job is to stack poison timers across opp's team. Cloyster has no recovery and is Water/Ice (not Steel/Poison-immune), so Toxic lands and puts it on a ~6-turn clock. Even if p1's Cloyster sets Spikes, it now has to spend turns recovering OR get worn down faster than it can convert. Toxic > Counter when no attacker is incoming.
investigation.set_archetype: Status Blissey (Soft-Boiled + Toxic + Light Screen + filler)

lesson: Blissey's role is status-spreader: Toxic every new switch-in regardless of what they are. Counter is a reactive trap that only fires vs a CONFIRMED attacker. When opp brings in a setter (Cloyster) or wall, Counter is dead weight — Toxic is always live.
